era_context: The mid-17th century: European monarchies reasserting power after civil wars and revolutions; the Qing Dynasty consolidating control over China; the Ottoman Empire maintaining regional dominance; the Dutch Golden Age at its zenith; colonial competition intensifying globally.

events.title: Restoration of Charles II to the English throne
events.description: Charles II is restored to the English throne on 29 May 1660 (Old Style: 8 May 1660), ending the Commonwealth and Protectorate period (1649–1660). The Restoration is achieved through the Convention Parliament, which invites Charles to return from exile in France. This event marks the end of the English Civil War era and the re-establishment of hereditary monarchy, the House of Lords, and the Church of England. The Restoration is celebrated as a turning point in English history, though it also involves the execution of regicides and the restoration of confiscated lands.

sources.name: Clarendon, History of the Rebellion and Civil Wars in England

events.certainty_note: The date of 29 May 1660 (New Style) is precisely documented in multiple contemporary sources. The event is one of the most thoroughly recorded in 17th-century English history.

events.title: Peace of Oliva ends Northern War between Sweden, Poland, and Russia
events.description: The Peace of Oliva (signed 3 May 1660) concludes the Northern War (1655–1660) between Sweden, Poland-Lithuania, and Russia. Sweden retains control of the Baltic and gains territorial concessions; Poland-Lithuania loses significant territory; Russia is excluded from Baltic access. The treaty establishes Sweden as the dominant Baltic power and marks a major shift in Northern European geopolitics. The peace also involves the Treaty of Copenhagen (1660) between Sweden and Denmark, further consolidating Swedish hegemony.
events.certainty_note: The date of 3 May 1660 is precisely documented in the treaty itself and contemporary diplomatic records.

events.title: Royal Society of London founded; scientific revolution institutionalised
events.description: The Royal Society of London is formally founded on 28 November 1660 (though it traces its origins to informal meetings in 1645). The society receives its royal charter from Charles II in 1662, but its establishment in 1660 marks the institutionalisation of experimental natural philosophy in England. The Royal Society becomes the leading scientific organisation in Europe, promoting empirical investigation and mathematical reasoning. Key early members include Robert Boyle, John Wilkins, and Christopher Wren. The society's motto, 'Nullius in verba' (Take nobody's word for it), encapsulates the empirical ethos of the scientific revolution.

sources.name: Royal Society records and founding documents
Thomas Sprat, History of the Royal Society (1667)

events.certainty_note: The founding date of 28 November 1660 is documented in Royal Society records. The formal charter is dated 1662, but the society's establishment is conventionally dated to 1660.

events.title: Zheng Chenggong (Koxinga) captures Formosa (Taiwan) from Dutch
events.description: Zheng Chenggong (known to Europeans as Koxinga), a Ming loyalist commander, captures Formosa (Taiwan) from the Dutch East India Company (VOC) in 1660–1662. The siege of Fort Zeelandia (Dutch stronghold) begins in 1660 and concludes with Dutch surrender in February 1662. This event marks the end of Dutch colonial control of Taiwan and establishes the Zheng family as the dominant power in Taiwan, which they will hold until the Qing conquest in 1683. The event is significant for the history of Taiwan and for the decline of Dutch power in East Asia.
events.certainty_note: The siege of Fort Zeelandia is well-documented in both Dutch and Chinese sources. The event spans 1660–1662, with the formal Dutch surrender in February 1662.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor assumes throne; Qing Dynasty consolidates power
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor (r. 1661–1722) formally assumes the throne of the Qing Dynasty in 1661, but the transition begins in 1660 with the death of the Shunzhi Emperor. Kangxi's reign marks the beginning of Qing expansion and consolidation, including the suppression of Ming loyalists and the integration of southern China into Qing control. The Kangxi Emperor becomes one of the most significant rulers in Chinese history, presiding over a period of cultural flourishing and territorial expansion.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and official histories

events.certainty_note: The Kangxi Emperor's assumption of the throne is precisely documented in Qing imperial archives. The date is 1661, but the transition process begins in 1660.

events.title: Treaty of Copenhagen between Sweden and Denmark
events.description: The Treaty of Copenhagen (signed 27 May 1660) concludes the Dano-Swedish War (1657–1660) and is part of the broader Northern War settlement. Denmark cedes significant territories to Sweden, including Scania, Halland, and Blekinge. The treaty establishes Swedish dominance in the Baltic and Scandinavia. Combined with the Peace of Oliva, the Treaty of Copenhagen marks a major realignment of Northern European power in Sweden's favour.
events.certainty_note: The date of 27 May 1660 is documented in the treaty itself and contemporary diplomatic records.

events.title: Act of Indemnity and Oblivion passed by English Parliament
events.description: The Act of Indemnity and Oblivion is passed by the Convention Parliament in 1660, pardoning most participants in the English Civil War and Commonwealth period, with the exception of the regicides (those who signed Charles I's death warrant). The act is a pragmatic measure designed to facilitate the Restoration and prevent widespread recriminations. However, the exception for regicides leads to the execution of several prominent Commonwealth figures in 1660–1661, including Thomas Harrison and Michael Livesey.

sources.name: Act of Indemnity and Oblivion (parliamentary statute)

events.certainty_note: The act is precisely documented in parliamentary records. The date of passage is 1660, though executions of regicides continue into 1661.

disconfirming_evidence: The Restoration of Charles II is often portrayed as a triumphant return to legitimate monarchy, but modern scholarship has emphasised the contingency and fragility of the event. The Restoration was not inevitable; it depended on the specific political circumstances of 1660 (particularly General Monck's decision to support the Convention Parliament) and could easily have failed. Additionally, the Restoration's religious settlement is often oversimplified: the re-establishment of the Church of England did not resolve religious tensions, and the exclusion of Nonconformists from political power created ongoing conflict that would erupt in the Exclusion Crisis (1679–1681). The Royal Society's founding is sometimes presented as a clean break from medieval scholasticism, but modern historians have shown greater continuity between Renaissance natural philosophy and the society's empirical methods than was once recognised.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1660 is exceptionally rich for Western Europe (England, Scandinavia, the Dutch Republic) due to abundant diplomatic correspondence, parliamentary records, and personal diaries. The record is also strong for East Asia (China, Taiwan) based on imperial archives and Chinese chronicles. However, the sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic and social history is underrepresented. The surviving sources also reflect the perspectives of the victors (Charles II's supporters, Swedish expansionists) and may underrepresent the experiences of those displaced or disadvantaged by these events (regicides, Danish territories lost to Sweden, Dutch colonists expelled from Taiwan).

graph_edges.note: The Restoration of Charles II leads to the passage of the Act of Indemnity and Oblivion, which pardons most Commonwealth participants except regicides.
The Restoration of Charles II occurs in the same year as the founding of the Royal Society, both marking a shift toward new intellectual and political orders.
The Peace of Oliva and the Treaty of Copenhagen are part of the same Northern War settlement, establishing Swedish dominance in the Baltic.
The siege of Formosa by Zheng Chenggong begins in 1660 and concludes with Dutch surrender in 1662.
The transition of power in the Qing Dynasty in 1660 leads to Kangxi's assumption of personal rule in 1661.
